examples.title: Simple Linear Regression (Supervised)
examples.description: Demonstrates supervised learning for regression - predicting continuous values
examples.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression

# Sample data: House sizes and prices
X = np.array([[1000], [1500], [2000], [2500], [3000]])  # Features (size)
y = np.array([200000, 280000, 350000, 420000, 500000])  # Labels (price)

# Create and train model
model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X, y)

# Predict for new house size
new_size = np.array([[1800]])
predicted_price = model.predict(new_size)
print(f"Predicted price: ${predicted_price[0]:,.2f}")
print(f"Coefficient (slope): {model.coef_[0]}")
print(f"Intercept: {model.intercept_}")
examples.explanation: Linear Regression learns the relationship between house size (feature) and price (label) to predict prices for new sizes.

examples.title: K-Means Clustering (Unsupervised)
examples.description: Demonstrates unsupervised learning for clustering - grouping similar data points
examples.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Customer data: Annual Income and Spending Score
X = np.array([[15, 39], [15, 81], [16, 6], [16, 77],
              [17, 40], [17, 76], [18, 6], [18, 94],
              [19, 3], [19, 72], [20, 14], [20, 86]])

# Apply K-Means with 3 clusters
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=3, random_state=42, n_init=10)
kmeans.fit(X)

# Get cluster labels and centroids
labels = kmeans.labels_
centroids = kmeans.cluster_centers_

print("Cluster labels:", labels)
print("Cluster centroids:", centroids)

# Predict cluster for new customer
new_customer = np.array([[18, 50]])
predicted_cluster = kmeans.predict(new_customer)
print(f"New customer belongs to cluster: {predicted_cluster[0]}")
examples.explanation: K-Means groups customers into clusters based on income and spending behavior without predefined labels.

examples.title: Decision Tree Classification (Supervised)
examples.description: Demonstrates supervised classification - predicting discrete categories
examples.code:
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder

# Sample data: Weather conditions and Play decision
# Features: Outlook (sunny, overcast, rainy), Humidity (high, normal)
training_data = [
    ['sunny', 'high', 'no'],
    ['sunny', 'high', 'no'],
    ['overcast', 'high', 'yes'],
    ['rainy', 'high', 'yes'],
    ['rainy', 'normal', 'yes'],
    ['rainy', 'normal', 'no'],
    ['overcast', 'normal', 'yes'],
    ['sunny', 'high', 'no'],
    ['sunny', 'normal', 'yes'],
    ['rainy', 'normal', 'yes']
]

# Encode categorical variables
le_outlook = LabelEncoder()
le_humidity = LabelEncoder()
le_play = LabelEncoder()

# Prepare features and labels
X = [[row[0], row[1]] for row in training_data]
X_encoded = [[le_outlook.fit_transform([x[0]])[0], 
              le_humidity.fit_transform([x[1]])[0]] for x in X]
y = le_play.fit_transform([row[2] for row in training_data])

# Train decision tree classifier
clf = DecisionTreeClassifier()
clf.fit(X_encoded, y)

# Predict: New day with sunny and normal humidity
new_day = [[le_outlook.transform(['sunny'])[0], 
            le_humidity.transform(['normal'])[0]]]
prediction = clf.predict(new_day)
print(f"Play decision: {'Yes' if prediction[0] == 1 else 'No'}")
examples.explanation: Decision Tree classifies whether to play based on weather features using learned rules from labeled data.
examples.timeComplexity: O(nd log n) for training, O(d) for prediction

examples.title: PCA Dimensionality Reduction (Unsupervised)
examples.description: Demonstrates unsupervised dimensionality reduction for feature compression
examples.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Sample data: 4 features (will reduce to 2 principal components)
X = np.array([
    [2.5, 2.4, 0.5, 0.7],
    [0.5, 0.7, 2.4, 2.6],
    [2.2, 2.9, 0.2, 0.5],
    [1.9, 2.2, 0.8, 0.8],
    [3.1, 3.0, 1.1, 1.2],
    [2.3, 2.7, 0.9, 0.9],
    [2.0, 1.6, 1.5, 1.0],
    [1.0, 1.1, 0.5, 0.6]
])

# Standardize features (important before PCA)
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Apply PCA: reduce from 4 to 2 dimensions
pca = PCA(n_components=2)
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

print("Original shape:", X.shape)
print("Reduced shape:", X_pca.shape)
print("Explained variance ratio:", pca.explained_variance_ratio_)
print("Total variance explained:", sum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_))
print("\nReduced data:\n", X_pca)
examples.explanation: PCA reduces 4-dimensional data to 2 principal components while preserving maximum variance in the data.
